hints.text: **Hindi** (Devanagari: top line through each word, straight verticals) → central & northern regions
**Marathi** — Devanagari like Hindi, but sometimes uses **ळ** (not in Hindi)
**Punjabi** — softer letter shapes, top bar **broken** over some letters; distinct **ਨ ਲ ਅ** + soft-curve diacritic below letters
**Gujarati** — like Hindi but **no top line** over the words
**Bengali** — Devanagari-like but many letters with **left-facing triangles**
bullets: Same script/language as **Bangladesh** — check plates/camera if unsure

hints.text: **Assamese** — Bengali script plus extra characters, notably **ৰ ৱ**
**Kannada** — smooth curves + small circles; often a **squiggly tail** top-right (rarer in Telugu)
**Telugu** — very similar to Kannada, but often **checkmark-like diacritics** on top of letters
**Tamil** — mix of curves, straight lines and **90° angles**; dot-shaped diacritics (also common in Sri Lanka)
**Oriya** — curvy; wide **semi-circles covering letter tops** (mnemonic: looks like the skull emoji 💀)
**Malayalam** — simple curvy letters, many **upside-down U shapes**
**Manipuri (Meitei)** — many vertical lines and right angles; metal trident poles, sometimes small bars on the trident
**Mizo** — Latin script (most common non-English Latin language in Indian coverage); lush green valleys, eroded hillsides with many scattered small peaks (Meghalaya's ridge tops are smoother)
**Almost exclusively English** signage — the only region like this
**Yellow tuk-tuks with black roofs** → south (Telangana, Andhra Pradesh, Tamil Nadu); rare elsewhere
**Concrete holey poles** — Gujarat + Daman & Diu / Dadra & Nagar Haveli
bullets: Holey poles take precedence over poletop metas
Rarely spill into Maharashtra, Madhya Pradesh, Chhattisgarh
hints.uniq_note: Called 'unique to Gujarat (+Daman & Diu, DNH)' by Plonk It — but they can rarely be found in Maharashtra, Madhya Pradesh and Chhattisgarh, so confirm with script/soil.

hints.text: Poles **divided in two sections** (Brazil-style)
Metal poles with a **mesh pattern**; taxis often have a **yellow hood** or small yellow circle; Buddhist prayer flags (also Arunachal)
**Metal holey poles**; small **yellow pole stickers** (similar stickers rarely elsewhere); with Goa the most developed states — wealthy modern homes
**Thin grey metal poles** (often two close together) → all of the northeast + Himalayan Uttarakhand & Himachal Pradesh
Electricity counters **mint green** with window-like squares (rarely Haryana); small **window-shaped metal poletops** (Punjab & Haryana); **gurdwaras** (white domes, Khanda symbol) most common here
Trident poletop with **centre insulator directly on the pole top**, outer two on a crossbar below (sometimes Haryana; multi-bar variant exists)
**Upside-down triangle poletops** below the pole top (less common in Madhya Pradesh)
Metal bar shaped like an **upside-down A** slightly below the pole top
**Upside-down trapezoid connector** on poles (also Meghalaya, Tripura, Maharashtra; rare elsewhere) — only use when no other pole meta is present
Poletops attached with two vertical bars forming a **tall narrow rectangle**; variant with two crossbars + diagonal supports to the pole

**Northeast pole metas**: even + uneven poletop combos; '**<**'-shaped double diagonal bars (Meghalaya & Arunachal, occasionally Assam); two crossbars w/ 2 insulators each (Assam/Meghalaya/Mizoram — **>2 bars → Meghalaya or Mizoram**)
Trident poletop with the **middle bar tip bent 90°**
Poletop with a **short crossbar above a longer crossbar**
Pole/tree markings: **one black stripe between two white** stripes
Pole paint: **5+ thin black-and-yellow stripes**; orange & bright-green **buses**
Pole paint: exactly **4 thicker black-and-yellow stripes**; trees with **4 red-white stripes** (also Jharkhand, less often)

**Roof metas**: central = rounded, disorganised tiles; south = organised flat bright-orange tiles w/ middle ridge (esp. Tamil Nadu; darker brown further north); West Bengal = flat tiles w/ **diamond protrusion** + large corrugated **hip roofs** (also nearby Bihar/Odisha); northeast = metal roofs w/ small top triangle + houses of square panels
**Thatched huts** → east coast + northern plains (big hay mounds too)
bullets: UP → Odisha: thatch of **fine hay**; Odisha → Tamil Nadu: **dried palm fans**

hints.text: **Pine metas**: chir pines (smooth conical crowns, long needles) → Uttarakhand/Himachal/J&K/W-Arunachal; khasi pines (jagged, disorganised crowns) → Meghalaya & Manipur
**Cacti fences** (Euphorbia) → Gujarat, W-Maharashtra, S-Rajasthan

**Palm metas**: palmyra (lollipop shape) in numbers → south/east; areca (very thin trunk, small crown) → SW coast, northeast, Andaman & Nicobar; date palms → dry central regions; oil-palm plantations → Andhra panhandle (near Telangana border)
**Crop metas**: tea → northeast + far south (flat tea = Assam / NE-West-Bengal); sugarcane → around Uttar Pradesh + southwest; cotton → Gujarat & Maharashtra; canola → around Rajasthan
**Himalaya** = by far the tallest mountains; **Ladakh/N-Himachal** = extremely dry high-altitude desert; **northern plains** (Ganges valley) = fertile, extremely flat open fields
**Soil metas**: distinctly dark soil → Maharashtra + adjacent; red soil → SW coast + around Chhattisgarh/W-Odisha; sandy dry → W-Rajasthan
**Western Ghats**: tabletop ridgelines (plateaus, not peaks); N drier, S greener — rainy season turns everything green
**Boxy square cars** visible in mapped areas (Odisha variant shows a hood); northeast: fences of thin wooden sticks in a square pattern
Black **pole stickers with digits + a middle arrow**
Planned city: blue **sector signs**, round yellow marks on street lights, street signs with **numbers instead of names** (nearby SAS Nagar & Panchkula similar)
Separated **middle bus lanes** on large roads (people still drive in them)
**Black taxis with yellow roofs**
**Elevated railways** → Bengaluru, Chennai, Delhi, Hyderabad, Jaipur, Kochi, Kolkata, Mumbai
Roofs of **thin grey bricks**; same bricks/rocks in walls & stockyards
NW-Bihar/NE-UP: **white-painted clay huts**; central Bihar: wooden huts with a **cross pattern**

**Tree spotlights**: Japanese cedar (tall, narrow fluffy crowns) → Sikkim/WB border + Meghalaya; Himalayan cedar → NH34 Harsil–Gangotri + Shimla; Bhutan white pine (hanging needles) → Mechuka valley + Bhutan border (Arunachal)
**Doum palms** (palmyra-like but multi-branched)
hints.uniq_note: 'Almost exclusive' to Diu — a stray doum palm elsewhere is possible, so confirm with the coastal Portuguese-influenced townscape.

hints.text: Endangered **Indian pitcher plant** — exclusive to the Khasi Hills (roadside esp. NH206 north of Umlārem)
Western half far more **barren** (logging/erosion); NW of Dehradun: barren brown-grass hills, small cliff-side roads; **Chaukhamba** massif near Ukhimath: snow, three peaks, centre tallest
The **only NE-India spot with visible snowy mountains**; brownish grass + Bhutan white pines; north of Mechuka increasingly overcast/dark

South of Shillong: **V-shaped green eroded valleys** with flat smooth ridgetops (Mizoram = less smooth, more rises/falls); NH6/NH206 SE of Shillong: khasi pines + **yellow outer road lines** + fresh dark pavement (only Meghalaya road with yellow outer lines)
**Salt flats & coastal marshes**, near-zero vegetation, shallow water pools (W-Rajasthan is sandier with desert shrubs)
Southern tip: huge **monolithic rock mountains** (Magenthiragiri hills visible from afar); flat bushy landscape with **very red soil** in the south
Abundant **coconut trees**, often painted **red-and-white** (like the Polish flag)
**NH326** west of Brahmapur: thin grey antenna + unusually high-quality shitcam (best near Harabhangi Reservoir)

videos.title: How You Know You're in Indonesia!
How to recognize Indonesia in GeoGuessr

hints.text: Plates **black with white text** — usually **three white sections** through the blur (commercial = yellow; newer coverage can be white)

shared_with.note: Malaysian plates are black too — but generally only **two** white sections.
shared_with.diff: Count the sections: 3 → Indonesia, 2 → Malaysia. Language spelling helps too (televisi vs televisyen).

hints.text: Drives on the **left**

shared_with.diff: Plate sections + all-white Malaysian road lines.

shared_with.note: The Philippines drives on the **right** — an instant separator.

hints.text: Centre lines **white or yellow** (solid or dashed); outer lines always white — yellow centre is rare in Gen3

shared_with.diff: Any yellow centre line → Indonesia, not Malaysia.

hints.text: Poles **round**, steel or concrete; steel often painted **black**, many with **Indonesian flag colours** (red/white); pole tops even or uneven by region
**Black-and-white** square/circular bollards + **yellow-and-black** ones (sometimes red reflector)
bullets: UK-style bollards mostly in **Kalimantan**

hints.text: **Yellow waystones angled towards the road** with a cutout near the top (3 town shorthands, biggest on top)
hints.uniq_note: Plonk It calls these 'unique to Indonesia'. The Philippines has waystones too — but **perpendicular** to the road with text facing away, so the angled orientation is the tell.

hints.text: **Indonesian** looks like Malaysian, but was influenced by **Dutch** (Malaysian by English): televisi (≈ Dutch televisie) vs Malaysian televisyen

shared_with.diff: Dutch-style spellings (-si, -tas) → Indonesia; English-style (-syen, -titi) → Malaysia. Plates: 3 vs 2 white sections.

hints.text: Rows of **red-and-white banners** everywhere (sometimes more colourful flags)
**Cigarette ads** everywhere — black/red schemes, ~18,000 Rp, graphic pictures, the word **PERINGATAN**
Motorcycles have **front licence plates** (unlike the rest of SE Asia)
hints.uniq_note: Within covered Southeast Asia, front plates on motorcycles are an Indonesia tell — other SEA countries' bikes don't carry them.

hints.text: Currency = **Rupiah (Rp)** on price signs
**Coverage map**: no Papua coverage; sparse in Kalimantan, Aceh, Central Sulawesi, North Maluku
**Kabupaten** (regency, 'Kab.') names on signs/billboards everywhere — 417 covered ones, learnable
Cardinal directions in addresses: Utara=N, Timur=E, Selatan=S, Barat=W, Tengah=Central, Tenggara=SE
**Satellite dishes** point toward mid-West-Kalimantan → tells you N/S of the equator + rough side of the country (angle ≈ distance)
**Postal codes** (5 digits) group by first digit: 1xxxx Jakarta → 9xxxx Maluku/Papua
Pole tops **even vs uneven** (symmetry of the insulator bar) — the base region-guessing meta
Poles with an **extra-long support** on the pole top (sometimes two); drier, low shrubby vegetation (Java can look similar — double-check)
Poles with a **triangle attachment** under the horizontal bar → mapped regions (not valid on stacked poles)
Poles with an **indent on both sides**
**Trident pole tops** (rarely S/W-Kalimantan, N-Sumatra, Riau)
Pole tops with an **extra attachment right under the horizontal bar**
Even bar with **uneven insulators** (1 vs 2) — almost exclusive to North Sumatra
**A-frame pole supports** (also Nusas) — caution: A-frames appear anywhere wires bend; green paint on bridge sides → S/Central Kalimantan

Crossbar with the **flat part facing away** from the pole (normally it faces the pole); painted **white+blue/red fences**; Central Sulawesi: unique **black-white-yellow bollards** + thin black secondary poles with light-blue/green tape (East Peninsula)
**Rectangle attachment** on pole tops (mostly Bali, with the Nusa support beam); pole paint with **horizontal text** (usually dark blue, also light blue/green)
Asymmetrical poles with **2 supports below the bar** (rare in Kalimantan/Java)
**Oil-palm plantations** → Sumatra & Kalimantan (rare in Sulawesi); **rice** → Java (some S-Sulawesi & S-Sumatra)
Very **rounded green hills** with palm/sugarcane/corn clusters
**Savannah** landscape on north Sumba (Gen3 there = always white car); distinct **high-pitched roof peaks**
**Red soil with white topsoil** spilling onto the road

**Roof metas**: tiled roofs → Java (+S-Sumatra, Nusas, S-Kalimantan — there flatter/less rounded); plain sheet metal → north (Kalimantan, Sulawesi, Sumatra); two-level symmetric triangle metal roofs → mainly Sulawesi

**Bugis roofs**: horizontal slots inside the roof triangles (rarely E-Kalimantan/W-Nusa); **gable horns** common here too (also Riau, C/S-Kalimantan); around **Rante Pao**: unique elevated **Tongkonan** saddle-roof houses
**Hindu-Balinese architecture**: crown-like roof pinnacles everywhere, guardian statues, slit-drum towers, dark volcanic **andesite** gates/walls/carvings
Distinct **boat-shaped roofs** (Minangkabau); North Sumatra's version is less angled, without spires — most common near **Lake Toba**
Roofs made of **wooden chips**
Muslim-majority country — **churches/Christian signs** are a strong clue for the mapped (Christian) regions
**Gen3 antenna** coverage → mostly Java (esp. East Java; very rarely Denpasar/Bali); **black Gen3 car** → almost everywhere, most common in Sulawesi
**Moiré-pattern camera glitch** throughout Central Kalimantan
Mountains around the lake + **Samosir Island** in the middle; overcast coverage typical
**Karst formations** in the east, esp. along the highway to East Kalimantan (similar karst: S-Sulawesi, W-Sumatra)
**Palu Bay**: NW-facing bay north of Palu — mountains west, flat urban east
Steeply pitched **palm-leaf roofs** (SW of North Sumatra)
Rare cars: **white SUV with black bars + wooden roof plank** (east Bali); **black cab with men in the truck bed** near Jagasatru
All coverage = a **single trekker inside the Iraqi National Museum, Baghdad**
Camera is **'Gen 1.5'** — Gen2-like but worse quality, often blindingly overexposed; trekker blur has a well-defined **pointed** shape
The only outdoor part = a small **patio w/ a diagonal walkway + date palms**
Interior = **statues, steles, artefacts** and a few back rooms
Plates long **white front AND back** + blue strip left

shared_with.note: UK = white front but **yellow back**, mostly without the strip.

hints.text: **Yellow dashed outer road lines** — very common (essentially never in the UK)

shared_with.diff: NZ = Pacific vegetation + different signage; Ireland = hedgerows + Irish bilingual signs.

hints.text: **Only European country with yellow diamond warning signs** (regulatory signs stay red-border/white)
Signage **bilingual English + Irish (italics)** — places, words and phrases translated
Chevrons **yellow-on-black** (UK: white); yield sign says **YIELD** (UK: GIVE WAY)
Pole warning sticker: **lightning bolt but NO man being zapped** + often a second outlined rectangular sticker (UK sticker shows the man)
**Green-white bollards** (not fully unique but great in UK 50-50s); rural gates w/ **rounded corners** (UK: square — but N. Ireland also rounded!)
**Boreens** — tiny rural roads w/ a grass strip down the middle (strong in UK 50-50s); drives **left**; smallcam Gen4
**Cream-white buildings w/ black roofs**, sometimes grey corner stones; row buildings in cities (British brick still occurs)

Very green agricultural land + **hedgerows** — often indistinguishable from the UK; wooden poles w/ **pole steps** + occasional crossbars (also UK/SE/FI); **double yellow no-parking lines** (also UK/Malta); B&W poles w/ yellow bulb at crossings (like UK)

shared_with.diff: Decide via: chevron colour, YIELD, plate backs, pole stickers, gate corners, diamond signs.

hints.text: Regional roads 3-digit: **first digit = region, anti-clockwise from Dublin** (8/9 = newer, non-regional; ~half of the 8s = Dublin metro); **area codes grouped by first digit**

**West coast**: rugged hills, wild brown-yellow moorland, exposed rock (vs neat green east); **Donegal**: tiny hilly rural roads w/ Gen4; **Wicklow Mountains** (S of Dublin) look western — but never a coast view + Gen4 small roads
**EV Gen4 car** (grey back w/ black semicircle) → most of the east + parts of the NW (Dublin also has the normal blue car)
**Tabletop mountains** → N-Sligo/Leitrim; **Burren NP** = grey limestone karst; **Cork** = the hilly big city
British Crown dependency between the UK & Ireland (counts as UK); coverage is only **Gen2** (lower quality, washed-out)
Plates UK-style (white front / yellow rear) but with a **red strip on the left**
Landscape = **hilly farmland** w/ narrow roads; drives on the left
**Whitewashed houses** w/ entrance walls (like Ireland); striped kerbs along main roads
**Blue bollards** common in urban areas
**Gorse** (bright yellow flowers) very common; Douglas = capital w/ terraced rows; lamp-post stickers narrow you to 1 of 21 parishes
Israeli plates **yellow with blue strip left** (also common in the West Bank)
hints.uniq_note: Yellow+blue-strip plates are specific to Israel — Jordan and the UAE use white plates, Tunisia black; but the design also dominates in the West Bank (same page here).

hints.text: Most Gen3: **black car with long antenna** (also black w/o antenna, silver w/ short antenna)
**Hebrew** (practically only here) + **Arabic** (esp. West Bank)
Landscape: greener **Mediterranean north** → very **arid south**
Bright, warm **stone buildings**, blocky; orange tiled roofs commoner than elsewhere in the Middle East, flat roofs equally common
Most roads: **yellow outer + white centre lines**; striped kerbs everywhere (red-white, black-white; rarely red-yellow, blue-white)

shared_with.note: Yellow outer lines also in UAE/Jordan/Oman.
shared_with.diff: White plates + desert + Gulf architecture → UAE; Hebrew → Israel.

hints.text: Both **A-type and B-type guardrails** common

shared_with.note: The only other Middle-Eastern country where B-type guardrails are common.

hints.text: Chevrons **white on black**; km markers white with thick **red/green/blue border**; stop sign shows a **hand** instead of a word
hints.uniq_note: The hand stop sign is a strong tell — but check for Hebrew to be safe (a few other countries use symbol stop signs on private roads).

hints.text: Poles: **black-white striped painted bottoms** + very common **mesh-framework poles**; yellow warning sign on poles
**Coverage**: sparse in southern Israel + most of the West Bank; **area codes** map (only mapped codes are regional)
**Gen4 only in Israel** (black car); mostly a **dirty front camera** — clean camera ≈ Tel Aviv area

**North** = mountainous, green, forested; **centre** = flat, urban, better highways; **south + eastern West Bank** = extremely dry/sandy; **west of Jerusalem** looks like the north — but always hilly (no wide flat valleys) + Jerusalem-stone houses; **West Bank** = semi-arid Mediterranean, hillier & less agricultural than central Israel
**Jerusalem stone**: nearly every building in Jerusalem + nearby West Bank cities (smoother on new buildings; single such houses exist elsewhere)
**Date-palm plantations** near the Jordanian border; cemented **rock walls** in the north; small **irrigation canals** around Beit She'an

**Water bodies**: Dead Sea = surrounded by total dryness (also visible from Jordan/West Bank); Sea of Galilee = noticeably vegetated; **Mount Carmel** = long NW-SE ridge near Haifa (green, high-altitude coverage on top); **Mount Gilboa** = very dry from afar, green on top
**West Bank plates**: white w/ green text (blurred = green tint); taxis/buses inverted (green w/ white); Israeli plates also common there
**West Bank follow cars**: small white Suzuki → centre/south; dark-blue Ford → northwest; big white Citroën 'SECURITY' → around Jerusalem (Bethlehem, Ramallah; always with antenna-less black car)

**Road/mountain spotlights**: Road 90 (longest) = dry + N-S angle (≈ roads 31/40!), southern stretch has a dry range east + palms; Road 2 = Mediterranean visible west (mountains east → Haifa↔Zikhron Yaakov); Mount Tavor = soft round shape; Timna NP = total dryness + ridges all around; tall rocky mountains east of **Eilat**; camel-back range → Kiryat Shmona; reservoir north of Hasolelim forest; tall coastal chimneys north of Netanya
Roadsides **full of parked cars** (free spots → probably the north of the city)
White rectangular signs with a **thin blue border**

**Jericho**: date palms in sparse, poor-looking urban setting; **Ramallah**: sheer mass of black water tanks on most houses; **Bethlehem**: blue house numbers w/ Arabic + Eastern-Arabic numerals split by a diagonal

**City-unique infra**: Harish grey mosaic tower brickwork; Nahariya 3-arm white lamps; Acre 'Barad-dûr' lamps (central-Israel lookalikes lack the horn gap); Netivot angular high+low lamps; Eilat blue-white pole bottoms; Kiryat Motzkin yellow fences/posts; Ashdod blue-white fences; Raanana thick/cube roundabout signs; Rahat grey Hyundai follow car
**House-number metas**: Rosh Haayin = blue w/ **G-clef**; Ness Ziona = Israeli flag; Herzliya = blue shield shape; **Bnei Brak** = ultra-Orthodox religious clothing on the street
**Israel National Trail**: one of the world's longest trekkers, through every landscape; orange-blue-white **trail markings**
Plates: **blue strips both sides + distinctly short front plate** — the combination is unique
hints.uniq_note: Albania also has two blue strips and France a small right strip — but neither has the short front plate; short front + two strips = Italy.

hints.text: Bollards **triangular, white w/ black top**, red front / white rear reflector

shared_with.diff: Albanian language + Gen4 quality + poorer roads → Albania.

hints.text: Very common: **solid white outer lines with NO middle line** (rarer in other Mediterranean countries)
**Italian**: double consonants, many i's, ì é à, words end in vowels; street = **via**; 'Passo carrabile' no-parking signs w/ comune name on top
Mediterranean: farmland on rolling hills + forested mountain valleys
Pastel stone houses w/ **terracotta roofs**; wooden **two-part sideways-opening shutters**
Many **round concrete poles** (unusual for the Mediterranean); wooden poles w/ small white sticker; **concrete trident poletops**
**Double guardrails** very common; guardrails w/ **red front reflectors** (Spain: yellow); distinct endings — flatter, wider, bending away
**Black/dark-grey sign backs** common

shared_with.diff: Romanian ă î ș ț → Romania.

hints.text: Directional signs blue w/ white arrow, **ALL-CAPS**; Strada Statale = blue w/ white-blue border; Autostrade = green; km markers = white squares w/ **roman numeral (100 m) over arabic (km)**; road numbers SS/SR/SP (State/Regional/Provincial)
hints.uniq_note: The roman-numeral km markers and SS/SR/SP codes are Italy-specific; the blue directional signs look like several neighbours'.

hints.text: Chevrons **white on black** (also ES/GR/AL); street signs white w/ thin blue border; pedestrian sign 5 stripes, person **without belt**
Gen4 **smallcam** coverage
**Area codes**: first digit increases east, then south; each region has its own **flag**
**South Tyrol** = German bilingual signs; **Aosta Valley** = French on signs (via + rue); **Slovene** bilingual near the Slovenian border
**Po Valley**: completely flat + notorious **haze** (worst air in Europe); rice → west/central Po (+ some Sardinia)

**Bari peninsula**: olive plantations + bright rock walls + red dirt (SE-Sicily similar); **Alps** = tall ranges + deep valleys; **Sicily** = arid hilly farmland + rocky mountains; **Sardinia** = grey rocks sticking up (esp. NE; Sicily drier); cacti → south (Sicily, S-Sardinia, S-Apulia); citrus → E-Sicily + S-Calabria

**Architecture regions**: Tuscany = irregular unpainted brown-grey brick (NE-Spain similar); Alpine = white stone + wood; Aosta = grey uneven brick (Andorra-like); Veneto/NE = solid shutters w/ black hinges; Apulia = light flat-roofed 2–3-storey houses (Middle-East vibe); **trullo cone roofs = Itria valley only** (Alberobello)
**Campione d'Italia** (exclave in Switzerland): low-cam + long antenna + flag, water to the west (antenna also in parts of CH)
**Volcano/city spotlights**: Etna towers over E-Sicily; Vesuvius S/E of Naples' plains; **Venice** = canals; Plana del Fùcino = mountain-ringed plain w/ perfect N-S road grid

**Island spotlights**: Pantelleria = dark volcanic rock walls + yellow lichen + cacti; Aeolians = volcanic + Mediterranean mix, white tourist villas (Salina = twin-volcano valley; Vulcano = white dirt stripe on the cone); Favignana = N-S mountain spine + Bari-like rock walls, NOT volcanic; San Domino = gloomy overcast **Gen2 only**

videos.title: How to recognize Japan in GeoGuessr

hints.text: Land terms to learn: 4 main **islands** (Hokkaido, Honshu, Shikoku, Kyushu) → **regions** (prefecture groups) → **47 prefectures**
Google car black-and-white (rarely black / with antenna) + **low-cam**: camera set low — bigger blur below, road looks wide, low perspective

shared_with.note: The only other country that ALWAYS has low-cam.
shared_with.diff: In Switzerland the **car itself is blurred**; landscape/language obviously differ.

shared_with.note: Gen4 Sri Lanka also uses low-cam.
shared_with.diff: Sinhala script + tropical island → Sri Lanka.

hints.text: Drives on the **left**

shared_with.diff: Traditional characters + English + dense high-rises → HK.
Right-hand traffic + Hangul (circles/ovals in script) → Korea.
Right-hand traffic + traditional characters + scooter armies → Taiwan.

hints.text: Writing mixes **kanji** (Chinese-borrowed) + **hiragana** (simple, loopy — loops don't exist in Chinese) + **katakana** (geometric, for loan words/ads)

shared_with.diff: Any **hiragana loops** (の, し, つ) → Japan. Mainland China is tripod-only coverage.
Hiragana → Japan; HK adds English everywhere.

hints.text: Frequent **kanji in place names** + endings: 県 prefecture, 市 city, 区 ward, 町 town; cardinal directions 北 N, 南 S, 東 E, 西 W — learnable, speeds up finding cities
Architecture very distinct & varied: traditional country houses, regional designs, modern cities, reinforced coastal buildings
Round concrete poles with **screw-like extensions**; carry electrical plates, guy-wires, reflectors → key region-guessing assets
**Pole reflectors**: short, don't touch the ground — yellow vertical/diagonal or white stripes

shared_with.note: Taiwanese reflectors have diagonal stripes that **reach the ground**.

hints.text: Plates short **white with green text**; yellow/black = **Kei cars** (light automobiles)
Road lines: white outer + dashed white middle (most common) / solid yellow middle / yellow+white middle — **never yellow outer lines**
Simple **white bollards with circular reflector(s)** on top (sometimes black or with extensions; may carry city/prefecture kanji)
Simple **white guardrails** standard
Prefecture roads = **hexagonal shields** (prefecture kanji under the number; same number exists in several prefectures → find the prefecture first)
Common infra: unique **inverted-triangle stop sign**, school pedestrian crossing sign, **traffic mirrors** everywhere
**Retaining walls** — excellent Japan clue in information-poor rural mountains
**Area codes increase to the south** (codes ending in 0, esp. 0120, are non-regional)
Per-region **pole plates**, regional **reflector pads**, prefecture **road diamonds**, prefecture **signpost stickers**, regional **guy-wires** — the five core pole/sign metas (maps)
**Pole attachments by region**: Chugoku = orange arrow above top plate; Shikoku = orange-black bands + small 90° attachments; Kansai = ~120° attachments (or angled-down)
**Transformer metas by region**
bullets: **Hokkaido**: plus-shaped side bar or 1 transformer on two bars; several = sides of a square; never a wooden plate
**Tohoku**: square holding brackets + Tohoku Electric logo; round plate w/ zigzag bar; square w/ bar on TOP (Niigata uses this design too)
**Kanto**: mounted on a **wooden plate** + two bars, or round plate + T-bar
**Hokuriku**: thin metal bars, small 'lamp' insulators, no bar
**Chubu**: yellow painted numbers + black 'tap' connectors; big triangle+horizontal bars or thin brackets
**Kansai**: square with bar on the BOTTOM (vs Tohoku top)
**Chugoku**: cross bar mid-pole on a FULL triangle; white sticker w/ red numbers
**Shikoku**: cross bar on a HALF triangle, hat-shaped insulators; sticker w/ black numbers
**Kyushu**: straight side bar w/ 3 insulators; transformers 180° from each other
**Okinawa**: T-shaped side bar; transformers on opposite pole sides

hints.text: Grey **insulator-like blocks** (wires fed through) → Chubu, Kansai, Chugoku, Shikoku (very rarely Hokkaido)
**Pole tops**: 4 thick bars + circle → Chubu; filled 'tent' → Hokuriku; 4 thin bars → Chugoku; tuning fork w/ long top bar → Kanto; short top bar → Chubu; thin wire → Kansai
**Snow shoulder arrows** hang over roads (almost exclusive here)
bullets: Aomori variant: silver bolts around the border (rarely Hokkaido)
Gifu variant: long & thin
NH275 north: yellow-black design; SW of Sapporo: yellow-green

hints.text: **Bollard metas by prefecture/region**
bullets: **Kyushu + Yamaguchi**: flat white, orange reflectors on ONE side (Hokkaido rare variant = both sides)
**Shikoku**: black stripe under the reflector
**Tohoku**: thick white + octagonal reflector + yellow stripe (black variants on national highways); striped covering bollards esp. **Akita**
**Akita**: 2 yellow stripes under octagonal reflector
**Toyama**: extendable, 1 reflector + RED stripe (2 reflectors or yellow stripe = generic!)
**Okayama**: octagonal reflector + red stripe + sloped base — exclusive
**Aomori**: extendable, 2 yellow stripes + circular reflector
**N-Chugoku**: basic thin snow poles (also Iwate)

hints.text: **Guardrail metas**: green bands → Oita/Miyazaki (all Kyushu); yellow guardrails → Yamaguchi; chevron-like stickers → Hokkaido; red-yellow end stickers → Chugoku except Yamaguchi (rarely Niigata)
**Vertical traffic lights** → Hokkaido + northwestern coast (horizontal everywhere else)
Black signs with **two yellow circles** (also stacked or with arrows)
**Kerosene 'Claymore' tanks** + hot-water/propane tanks → more common the further north (abundant Hokkaido) & in high mountains; esp. rural towns
**Shosetsu** snow-melting roads (tiny circles in the pavement spraying warm water) → mainly northwest coastal prefectures
Cycling routes: **white outer + blue inner line** (mostly coastal; rare exceptions elsewhere)
**Red-tiled roofs** → Chugoku (esp. Hiroshima & Shimane); roofs get flatter/sloped further **north** (snow) + side ladders; **Okinawa** = white concrete flat-roof buildings
**Giant butterbur ('cabbage')** → abundant in Hokkaido + northern prefectures (smaller leaves further south)
bullets: Also on Sakhalin (Russia) — but no low-cam there

hints.text: **Crop metas**: green tea in hedged rows + frost fans → Shizuoka (36%) & Kagoshima (34%); rice → north of Tokyo (Tohoku 27%, Chubu 21%, Kanto 15%, Hokkaido 7%); sugarcane → S-Kyushu islands + Okinawa
**Vegetation metas**: Japanese white birch → Chubu & Hokkaido (some Tohoku; beech has grey spotted bark); bamboo → more south; Chinese silver grass → Kyushu (+Niigata/Yamagata/Akita)
Very limited **winter/snow coverage** — don't guess Hokkaido without certainty; mapped regions most likely
Very mountainous country — the recognisable flat exceptions: **Nobi & Kanto plains**
**Shisa guardian lions** + house plaques with specific characters; red-soil fields on Miyakojima & Ishigaki
**Mount Fuji** — tallest, gradual incline, snowy summit, visible from far → direction+distance = strong region guess
Manhole covers with a **5-petal flower** + small circles around
**Cyrillic on signs** in these two northern Hokkaido cities
Poles **painted as birch trees**
Huge number of **trekkers/tripods** across many prefectures (see Plonk It's dedicated resource)
British Crown Dependency in the Channel Islands (off NW France; counts as UK); all **Gen2**; winding hedgerow lanes, usually **sunny**
Plates UK-model (white front / yellow rear) + island **red coat of arms** + a **'J'** prefix; **no blue strip** (never in the EU)
UK-style infrastructure (drives left, chevrons, warning signs) but **French street & locality names** (Jèrriais on some signs)
Architecture like northern France: pastel walls + **dark slate roofs**; **red-granite** walls & houses; British terraced houses too
**Yellow telephone booths** (UK's are red); Cornish palms used as decorative plants
**Yellow give-way lines** at intersections (UK = double dotted white); '**Green Lane**' cycling signs + blue island-map route markers
Street signs carry the **parish coat of arms**; St Helier = urbanised capital; Les Mielles = treeless western coast
**Black car, no antenna, no red brake light**
hints.uniq_note: The only Middle-Eastern country covered by a black car without antenna or brake light — elsewhere in the world black cars exist, so confirm with Arabic-only signage.

hints.text: Plates **white** (long or short); taxis/commercial = **green strip left**

shared_with.note: UAE also uses long white plates.
shared_with.diff: The **green strip** is Jordan-only in the Middle East; UAE has English on most signs, Jordan doesn't.

shared_with.note: Israel = yellow plates; the West Bank mixes white domestic + yellow Israeli.
shared_with.diff: Yellow plates → Israel; Hebrew script → Israel.

hints.text: **Arabic** — and unlike the UAE, most commercial signs have **no English**

shared_with.note: Arabic too — but with English on most commercial signage.

hints.text: **Yellow outer + white middle lines**
Chevrons **white with red arrows** (Israel: black w/ white arrows)
Poles: rounded concrete w/ **diagonal support** or **3 horizontal Y-tops**; thin metal poles w/ 2 supports + 3 vertical coils
Very **arid**, much lifeless desert — even the lushest parts are only arid-Mediterranean
Bright **sandstone-brick buildings with flat roofs**

shared_with.note: Jerusalem & West Bank use similar sandstone.
shared_with.diff: Hebrew or yellow plates → Israel side.

hints.text: Road quality often poor — heat-warped, **buckled cracks** (generally worse than Israel)
Coverage only in the **far west**: bigger towns + main roads; ends N of Jerash (Irbid = zero coverage), south to Aqaba
Largest city w/ extensive coverage: big-city feel, slightly hilly, medium apartment blocks; taxis **yellow-green**
**Highway metas**
bullets: **15** (longest divided, N-S): desert; rocky hills near Aqaba
**35** (Amman↔Jerash, divided): green-speckled hills + **T-shaped lampposts** (only here)
**40** (west of Amman, divided): low green shrubs, hills → sandy Dead-Sea side; **Y-shaped lampposts**
**65 north**: Dead Sea directly west + long flat ridge across
**65 south**: single-lane N-S, long sandy stretches south of Feifa (some overcast; sunset near Aqaba airport)
**80**: divided valley road between rigid mountains, runs **E-W** (vs 15's N-S)

hints.text: Flat, sometimes tilled fields w/ **rich red soil** (continues N+S along Hwy 35); taxis yellow w/ **white bumpers**
**Wadi al Hasa** (Hwy 35): arid rolling-hill valley, smooth rounded rock, single-lane road; **Aqaba**: jagged mountain wall east/south + olive-green/black taxis
Wadi Musa: **bulbous rock formations**; Petra trekkers: eroded reddish rock, narrow gorges, tourist crowds (a mainstay of the default World map!)
Plates mostly **white with a small blue hint** on the left (when blurred)

shared_with.note: Russian plates are fully white — no blue at all.
shared_with.diff: Any blue hint left → Kazakhstan; camera: KZ = white pickup, RU = black/white car.

hints.text: **Kazakh** in Cyrillic + Latin (sometimes monolingual Russian); extra letters vs Russian: **Ә Ғ Қ Ң Ө Ұ Ү Һ І**

shared_with.note: Russian text appears monolingually in KZ too.
shared_with.diff: Spot any of **Ә Ғ Қ Ң Ө Ұ Ү Һ І** → Kazakhstan. White pickup camera confirms.

shared_with.note: Kyrgyz shares **Ө Ү Ң** — but not the rest.
shared_with.diff: **Ә Қ Ғ Ұ І** → Kazakhstan; silver 4-bar Gen3 car → Kyrgyzstan.

hints.text: ALL coverage = **white pickup truck** (fully visible or mostly blurred)
hints.uniq_note: Several countries have white pickup cameras (e.g. UAE Gen4 trekker-style) — but in a post-Soviet steppe context the white pickup means Kazakhstan.

hints.text: Huge & diverse — deserts, grassy steppes, mountain passes, birch forests; rural coverage overwhelmingly **empty, open, flat**
Infrastructure very **Russia-like** (ped signs, poles, houses, bollards); tree trunks often painted **white at the bottom**

shared_with.diff: White pickup + Kazakh letters + gas pipes everywhere → Kazakhstan.
Red plate strip + silver Gen3 car → Kyrgyzstan.

hints.text: Pedestrian crossings **yellow+white** — usually with **white dashes on either side** (Russia lacks the dashes; sometimes dashes only)

shared_with.note: Yellow-white crossings too — but without side dashes.

hints.text: Street signs on buildings: **blue with intricate white patterns** top & bottom, bi-/trilingual, often a **QR code** (designs vary within one city)
Post-Soviet + Turkish-style bollards; **two thin bollards joined at 90°** are unique to Kazakhstan
Chevrons **yellow-and-black** (unlike neighbours) — except T-junctions: generic red/white
**Gas pipes everywhere** in cities

shared_with.note: Above-ground pipes exist in Russia (and Georgia/Ukraine) too.
shared_with.diff: Density in Kazakh cities is far higher; white pickup confirms.

hints.text: **Koshkar-muiz** ornamental pattern in decorations everywhere; ornate **city/town entrance arcs**
Coverage limited: major roads + cities — knowing the covered roads narrows guesses a lot
**Season ≈ region**: summer = west & central · autumn = northeast · winter = southeast
**Driving-direction map** helps on rural roads; car-dirt metas: thin dust coat on the roof **Kokshetau–Atbasar**; vertical dirt line right of roof **east of Aktobe**
**Area codes** run east→west along 3 horizontal bands; truck/bus **plate codes** alphabetical (buses most reliable); QR-code letters on street signs = region
Generally **flat** → hills are a clue: Karaganda hilly / hills in view; hills around Aktobe; big mountains = **south or east**

**North**: lush birch forests + green fields (looks like Russia's Omsk Oblast!); **NW**: vast grassy plains (Akmola, Kostanay); **SW**: sandy shrubby desert; **SE**: tall snow-capped mountains + snow coverage east of Almaty

shared_with.note: The northern birch-field landscape is nearly identical to Omsk Oblast.
shared_with.diff: White pickup camera → Kazakhstan; Gen4 red/white car → Russia (Omsk).

hints.text: Hilly & forested, **October fall colours**; Ridder flat but ringed by hazy mountains; around **Semey**: pine clusters/forests + fall-coloured deciduous
Hilly, dry & shrubby with **orange grass**; dead-flat farmland near Zhetisay — the Kyrgyz-looking **red-white km markers** appear ONLY near Zhetisay; grassy green rolling hills just north of Bishkek
**Road metas**
bullets: E38 Shymkent→Kyzylorda: divided, all-white lines; green south → dry sandy north
M36 Karaganda→Almaty: under construction, yellow lines, orange-brown winter steppe (drives S from Karaganda)
M36 Karaganda→Astana: divided, **yellow outer lines**, grassy steppe, SE-NW angle
P4/A17 Astana→Pavlodar: divided E-W, less vegetated, yellow outer lines
Kyzylagash→Druzhba: hazy dead hills, small mountains S/E (drives east)
A26 Kandyagash→M32: terrible/unpaved road, cloudy, semi-arid flats (drives mostly north)
A1 Astana→Shchuchinsk: 6-lane divided, green trees & plains
A17 to Ulytau: crumbling edges, single white middle line; W of Atasu sun low on horizon; E of Atasu new red-tinted pavement
N of Zhezqazghan: no lines, desolate, red/orange soil, sunny w/ cumulus (drives N from Nikolskiy)
R260: cracked pavement, no lines, overcast, yellow grass + reddish soil (drives S from Atbasar); R217 similar but better road w/ faded dashes, clouds thin eastward

hints.text: **Pole paints** identify cities (top→bottom)
bullets: West Kazakhstan Region: red-white-black
Atyrau: blue-white-black
Akmola: thin red, white, thin black
Kostanay: thin turquoise + yellow
Petropavl: thin white + light grey
Taraz: red-yellow-black
Kyzylorda: thin yellow+red, then white

Also: Rudny red-yellow-red+white, Kokshetau light-blue bottom (+yellow strips), Shchuchinsk thin red+yellow, Ekibastuz thin blue+white, Shymkent yellow+red then green, Kentau blue/yellow/red + blue/black

hints.text: **Bus stops & buses** identify cities
bullets: Stops: Atyrau modern white+neon-green w/ name on top; Kostanay brown w/ blue-yellow crest; Petropavl brown, 3 glass sides, upturned roof; Karaganda old turquoise curved; Zhezqazghan wide blue 2-section

Buses: Aktobe orange-black; Zhezqazghan orange-white 'Men ♥ Jezz'; Astana white-black-green electric; Karaganda turquoise; Semey light green-white; Almaty full green w/ white pattern (Taraz similar but labelled 'Taraz')

hints.text: **Street-sign designs**: Aktobe = half QR code; Petropavl = white w/ blue semicircle; Astana = consistent generic; Zhetisay = generic (but absent in Turkistan/Shymkent)
**Lamps & boxes**: yellow gas-pipe boxes → Shymkent/Turkistan/Taldykorgan; thin grey twin-light lamps → Turkistan; blue photocell lamps → Kulsary; triple ascending lamps → Uralsk (unique)

**Spotlights**: dense baltic-pine forest N of Shchuchinsk; Oskemen hazy forest-fire coverage; snow in the mountains of S-Almaty City; **Altyn-Emel NP** barren grassland ringed by snowy mountains + curvy pass; the only **May coverage** = Alataou exclave E of Almaty (sunny, mountains south); Taldykorgan hill ridge to the N; near the Turkmen border: **blindingly bright sandy desert road**
Mostly **Gen4 pickup truck** (often blurred; sometimes a **snorkel** — only Kenya & Mongolia have Gen4 snorkels)
Road lines: **outer always solid white**, inner **yellow** (solid or dashed)
Poles concrete or wood with an **L-shaped crossbar** (a similar pole exists in Ghana)
Plates **long white front / square yellow rear**; a grey Toyota SUV often **follows** the Street View car
**Safaricom** (mobile-network brand) is unique to Kenya — almost every city has one
Directional signs **green w/ white border & text** (road numbers white/yellow); other road signs mostly red/white
**Repurposed vans** (matatus) found all over (Uganda's vehicle-wrap design differs)
Many **eucalyptus** species (long clumped leaves, smooth bark); Gen3 = visible roof rack + snorkel (or no car)
Pole markings often carry a **skull + danger** message
**County names on signs/billboards** → region; PO BOX **postcode** format (PO BOX XXX-**YYY**ZZ); east/north flat, SW higher; coastal coconut palms; red brick in the west + east of Nairobi

videos.title: 3 Levels of GeoGuessr Tips from a Pro - KYRGYZSTAN

hints.text: Plates have a **red strip**

shared_with.note: Albanian plates can also carry a **single red strip on the left** — the only European country with red on the plate.
shared_with.diff: Albania = Gen4 camera, Mediterranean landscape, **Latin** script; bollards with black top + red reflector.

shared_with.note: Very rarely, UAE plates show a red element too.
shared_with.diff: UAE = desert, **Arabic** script, white Gen3 car with black roof racks — nothing like snowy Central Asia.

hints.text: **Silver car with four bars** visible; all coverage is **Gen3**
hints.uniq_note: Other countries have similar silver Street View cars (e.g. **Guatemala**), but no country with a **similar landscape** has one — and none of them uses the **Cyrillic** alphabet.

hints.text: **Kyrgyz Cyrillic** — like Russian but with extra letters **Ө Ү Ң** (not in Russian Cyrillic)

shared_with.note: Kazakh Cyrillic ALSO has **Ө Ү Ң** — plus even more extras: **Ә Ғ Қ Ұ Һ І**.
shared_with.diff: Spot **Ә Қ Ғ Ұ І** → Kazakhstan. Camera: KZ = white pickup (often Gen4), KG = silver Gen3 car with 4 bars.

shared_with.note: Mongolian Cyrillic has **Ө Ү** too (but no Ң) and loves double vowels (аа, ээ, уу).
shared_with.diff: Double vowels or the **ЗОГС** stop sign → Mongolia; yurts + bowling-pin bollards seal it.

shared_with.note: Plain Russian Cyrillic — none of the Kyrgyz extra letters.
shared_with.diff: No **Ө Ү Ң** anywhere → think Russia (its distinctive letters are Ы Э Ё Ъ). Plates fully white, no red strip.

shared_with.note: Ukrainian Cyrillic instead has **Є Ґ І Ї** — including a Latin-looking i.
shared_with.diff: A Latin-looking **і** inside Cyrillic text → Ukraine, not Kyrgyzstan.

shared_with.note: Bulgarian is Cyrillic too, but easy to tell apart: signs very often mix **Cyrillic + Latin** transliterations.
shared_with.diff: Latin everywhere alongside Cyrillic + **EU blue strip on plates** (very common sight) → Bulgaria. Bulgarian also uses **Ъ** as a vowel mid-word.

hints.text: All coverage was captured in **winter**
bullets: Many trees are **leafless**, colours drab
**Snow** visible in many parts of the country

shared_with.note: Parts of Russia also have snowy, leafless Gen3 coverage.
shared_with.diff: Check the camera: KG = **silver car w/ 4 bars**; Russia = black/white Gen3 car w/ **long antenna** (or Gen4). Plates: KG red strip vs RU fully white.

hints.text: Trees & concrete poles painted **white at the bottom**

shared_with.note: White-painted trunks and pole bottoms are a **post-Soviet standard** — Russia is full of them.
shared_with.diff: Fully white plates + long-antenna Gen3 or Gen4 → Russia.

shared_with.note: Tree-trunk bottoms often painted white; infrastructure is very Russia-like.
Random objects incl. trees & poles painted white (or blue-and-yellow) are common.
shared_with.diff: Plate with **blue-yellow strip** on the left + some of Europe's worst roads → Ukraine.

hints.text: Simple **white bollards with a diagonal black stripe** near the top

shared_with.note: Russia uses masses of white/black-striped bollards at its unusually wide intersections.
shared_with.diff: Sheer bollard quantity + fully white plates → Russia; combine with the car meta.

shared_with.note: Mongolia's white **bowling-pin bollards** with two red stripes look similar from afar.

hints.text: km markers with a **red-and-white triangle pattern** are unique to Kyrgyzstan
Majority **Muslim** → many mosques throughout the country

shared_with.note: Kazakhstan is Muslim-majority too — mosques across very similar steppe/mountain towns.
shared_with.diff: White pickup camera car (often Gen4) + Kazakh extra letters **Ә Қ Ғ** → Kazakhstan.

hints.text: Coverage is **very limited** — learning the covered roads pays off massively
Learn the **Google-car driving direction** on the major roads to narrow down options
**Car-mirror metas** — the mirror region-guesses Kyrgyzstan
bullets: **Southern mirror**: less road visible in the mirror, car reflection more diagonal → Osh / Manas region
West of the roundabout up to Manas the southern mirror carries **varying dirt** ("Southern Dirty Mirror")
A **small black dot** on the mirror → the Sokuluk–Kara Balta belt and nearby northern roads

hints.text: **Divided roads** are concentrated in and around Bishkek; a major divided highway runs Bishkek → Balykchy
bullets: A few marked cities elsewhere also have at least one divided road

hints.text: **Lake Issyk-Kul** is the biggest body of water — often visible from the roads north & south of it
bullets: If buildings/trees block it, the **absence of mountains** toward the lake is the tell
Don't confuse with **Toktogul Reservoir** or **Lake Song-Kul** — distinguishable with practice

hints.text: **Northern roads**: considerable snow + generally bright sky
bullets: The south has snowy roads too — use the **mirror meta** to separate them

hints.text: **Bishkek** is by far the most developed city — many taller multi-storey buildings, fancier buildings on the outskirts; mountains to the south (often hard to see)
Urban **snow coverage** exists in one region of Bishkek
South of Bishkek: hilly, snowy **Tian Shan** to the south, visible **smog** to the north (most polluted city air)
Towns east & west of Bishkek feature **colourful poles** — yellow, green, white, sometimes red on top
bullets: **Sokuluk**'s poles lack the red top
Not to be confused with **Talas** (white bottom + red-and-yellow stripes)

hints.text: **Osh** = 2nd-biggest city; has the **southern mirror**; weather fair with some clouds — never fully overcast (that's Manas)
Trees in Osh mostly **keep their leaves** (vs leafless Bishkek)
In the centre you can see **mountains with radio masts** on top
**Manas** (prev. Jalal-Abad) = 3rd-biggest city; southern mirror; **extremely overcast** with no cloud gaps (vs Osh's fair weather); slightly snowy hill to the east
**Arslanbob** — snowy mountain resort town north of Manas; big elevation changes inside the town; southern mirror
**Uzgen** — slightly snowy town between Osh & Manas; overcast coverage; southern mirror
In **Talas** most poles are painted white at the bottom **with red & yellow stripes**
**Karakol** sits between snow-capped mountains north & south; sunny coverage; melting snow on the ground
**Naryn** — steep **orange ridge** to the north, big snow-capped mountains to the south
**Road metas below** use OSM road numbers (Google Maps mislabels them) — in-game, orient by town names
**EM-04** — the major road Bishkek → Osh
bullets: Sokuluk→Kara Balta: distinct **sunset** coverage (Black Dot Mirror)
Flat stretch with the **Tian Shan to the south** — the further away, the foggier they look (Black Dot Mirror)
Steep rocky cliffs w/ **purple/red tint**, only grass & shrubs; small river beside road; **brown metal pylons** + curved concrete guardrails (Black Dot Mirror)
Long stretch: snow everywhere **except on the road**; hilly, mountainous at the NE & SW ends (mostly Black Dot Mirror)
Mountain section: rocky cliffs with **spruces**, mixed trees along the river, snowy peaks; most signposts **blue**
**Dried-up part of Toktogul Reservoir** north or south of the road; tall mountains close on every side; N half desolate, S more vegetated
**Toktogul as an actual lake** north of the road; hilly (esp. south); road in better condition than the one south of Issyk-Kul
River gorge with large rocky mountains; **overcast**; the Naryn river usually visible to the north
Southern flats: lots of agriculture & roadside vegetation; hills close to the N/E (Southern Dirty Mirror)
NW of Uzgen: **overcast + lots of snow** (Southern Mirror)

hints.text: **EM-11** (A365 on Google Maps) — Bishkek down to the Chinese border
bullets: Divided start: **grassy median** until Khun-Chi
Khun-Chi → E of Tokmok: **wide-gap double road lines** as the median
The gap thins further east, until past Kemin
Then **concrete barriers** as medians
Undivided south of Balykchy: extremely dry shrubland (greener along the **Chu river**); clouds ONLY above the northern mountains
Dry valley flanked by mountains, shrubs only; slightly **snowy ridge to the north** in the southern half
Next valley: rocky mountains **with trees**; very bad pavement → **dust clouds**
Out of the valley: greener, more buildings & animals on grassy fields; **snowy mountains south**; hills N of Naryn dry but snow in the shade
South of Naryn: unique mix of **snow + red soil**
**At-Bashi valley**: distinct uniform range to the south, scattered mountains north, big hills west

hints.text: **EM-09** — Balykchy → Karakol along the NORTH shore of Issyk-Kul
bullets: Lake usually visible to the **south**; car drives **east** (last stretch south)
Balykchy → Cholpon-Ata: **sunny**, mountains north + lake south
East of Cholpon-Ata: **overcast**, tall tree rows along the road
Vertical stretch north of Karakol: tall tree rows; snowy mountains N & S; the weather flips around the midpoint

hints.text: **EM-10** (A363) — Karakol → Balykchy along the SOUTH shore of Issyk-Kul
bullets: Lake now visible to the **north**; car drives **west**
West of Karakol: lush roadside vegetation, green/yellow fields; Tian Shan far north, big mountains south; weather fully clear east of one intersection
Middle: lake + Tian Shan almost always in view; **sandy/rocky soil**
West of "Fairytale Canyon": distinct **sunset** coverage
Mountains & hills south, low hills north with seemingly **nothing behind them**; lots of roadside sand
Western lake end: driest part; lake & hills sit further from the road

hints.text: **EM-17** — southeast of Talas
bullets: Very snowy hills & mountains; road often covered in uncleared **slush/melted snow** — unlike the other snowy roads (Black Dot Mirror)
Further west: only the southern hills snowy; otherwise dry, hills N & S (mostly Black Dot Mirror)

hints.text: **EM-13** — small gravel road near Kazarman (only bits covered)
bullets: East of Kazarman: unpaved gravel between two hilly ridges; the northern ridge is further away with bigger mountains behind — incl. one **towering peak**
Eastern side resembles the road west of Naryn, but unpaved & hills closer to the north; bushes, shrubs, grass
The one town in the region: **white-and-red fences with a white diamond pattern**

hints.text: **M-072** — west of Naryn, south side of the dry Naryn valley
bullets: Hills close on the **south** side with bigger mountains behind; wide plain to the north with distant hills
Pavement often has a **salt-and-pepper** look

hints.text: **M-068** — east of Naryn, also in the Naryn valley
bullets: Hills close to the south (with **some snow**), further away north; the northern plains are smaller
More built up than M-072; the road itself is unpaved in parts

hints.text: **M-082** — Song-Kul and the mountains south of it
bullets: **Song-Kul** = the 2nd major lake; dry rolling hills with snow patches; the only covered road is **gravel**
South of Song-Kul: the only unpaved road this high up — **clouds below you**, rugged peaks, snowy cliff sides, spruces

hints.text: **M-118** — Arslanbob down into the Fergana valley
bullets: **Kara Darya river** east of/below the road; hilly & green; low snowy mountains far north (Southern Dirty Mirror)
Northern part: snow on the ground (more the further north); massive **Gora Babash-Ata** mountain to the north (Southern Dirty Mirror)

hints.text: **M-107** — Taskomur → Kerben (only the first half covered)
bullets: Dry & rocky; one specific **ridge** is visible from most of the section
Further west: very hilly & lush (tall grass, trees, bushes); **concrete-barrier guardrails with cement above the gaps**; drives SE → NW

hints.text: **Arkit road** — up to Sary-Chelek lake, one of the most unique roads
bullets: Vegetated valley with many buildings & fences near the road; poor, unpaved, potholed; darker clouds north — cloudier + more dead trees toward Arkit; drives **S → N**
Near Arkit: snow everywhere + low broad-leaved trees — paired with the S→N direction, very easy to recognise

hints.text: **Other smaller covered roads**
bullets: M-010: spruce-covered snowy mountains; snow gathered at the road sides
Chunkurchak dirt road (S of Bishkek): rocky, partly **red-tinted soil**, snowy hills south; **red-and-white bollards**
Road east of Chunkurchak: rocky valley, varied mountainside plants, patchy snow (more in the south)
A367 (EM-18): snow-covered ranges in every direction; **blinding sun to the south** (Black Dot Mirror)
Airport road N of Bishkek (EM-14): divided, **yellow-and-black median curbs**, grassy median with planted trees; poles with red+yellow bottom paint

How to tell USA & Canada apart
Asking GeoGuessr Devs Their Best USA Tips

hints.text: Speed signs say **"SPEED LIMIT"** (Canada = "MAXIMUM"); **miles**, feet/inches; limits often end in 5
Wordy signs (railway crossing spelled out); **US English** (center, color)
**"No Passing Zone"** yellow triangle = US only; rectangular junction signs (Canada = diamond)
Yellow centre + white outer lines; **double yellow** common (v. rare in Europe)
White+orange **fibre-optic markers** ~unique to US; rumble strips common
Trailer/mobile homes rural (esp. SE); **Baptist churches** common
US+Canada: post mailbox w/ flag; wooden poles + **cylindrical transformers**; short plates (no blue strip); overhead signal bars; long-nose semis (Europe = cabover)
Gen2 rare → if Gen2 in N. America likely **Canada** (except Alaska, some Hawaii); Gen3 mirror = **single peak** (Canada = two peaks); NA Gen3 usually **no antenna**
East of Missouri R. = denser pop. & greener; West = mountains, drier
Deep South (OK→SC) **reddish soil**; SW Four Corners red mesas/tablelands
PNW (WA/OR/N.CA coast) **temperate rainforest**; Great Plains flat, few trees
New England architecture (shingle/wood panel, porch, high roof) + **three-deckers** → Northeast
Interstates: **even = W–E** (low #s at Gulf/Mexico), **odd = S–N** (low #s at West Coast); US Highways numbered inversely
**No front plate** → rear-only states skew **South & East** (front-plate states are mostly West/Midwest); each state also has a unique plate design (some readable through blur)
3-digit **area codes** (not clustered but learnable); each state has its own **flag** (distinct ones appear often)
Appalachians (low forested mtns) → east; Rockies → MT–NM; Great Basin (mtns + dry shrubby plains) → NV/UT
**Saguaro cacti** (tall tubes w/ branching arms) → southern Arizona, Sonoran border
Utility poles w/ **3 yellow stripes** at the base (most of CA, exc. Modoc/Lassen NE)
**Front Range**: a N–S mountain ridge flanking Denver/Boulder/Colorado Springs, plains to the east
Central FL (Orlando↔Lakeland) = the only real **rolling hills**, lakes + groves; jungle-straight roads far south
**Square-reflector bollard** (also UT/AZ) — Idaho appears with both 1 and 2 bolts
**Concrete paving** on ordinary rural roads much more common than elsewhere (ignore bridges/highways/cities)
**White reflectors** on rural signposts (esp. stop signs) — unique to Kansas
Uniquely-shaped **rumble strips**: thin, long recessed lines on the very outside road edges
**Shotgun houses** (very narrow 1-storey wood) → New Orleans area (also Houston, Louisville, Mississippi)
Stoplights & signs **hang from wires** across big intersections, often in yellow boxes
**Yellow signs w/ vertical black triangles** before bridges at major river crossings & guardrail ends
County highways use a **letter system (A–ZZZ)** — Missouri signs lack the word 'county' (Wisconsin has it)
**Sandhills**: rolling grass-covered dunes in the central-north of the state
Bollard = **white vertical-rectangle reflector w/ black outline** on a thin stick
White metal **"E-2" sign** beside the road, typically at bridge ends
Bollard with **3 circular reflectors**; Pueblo-Revival adobe (flat roofs, rounded, sand-brown) buildings
**Square guardrails** (long top bar on square legs) dominant here (also Wyoming)
Street signs showing **street numbers + arrows** alongside the name
**Green sign, 3 rows of numbers** on the back of rural stop signs at intersections
Speed signs often say just **"SPEED"** (not "SPEED LIMIT"); bollard = very long rectangular reflector on top
White intersection signs w/ the **converging state-route number** on top; **keystone** route markers
**White diamond reflector** bollard, usually **2 bolts** (Montana = 1 bolt); Black Hills (conifers) far west
**Yellow/red bands** ('ketchup & mustard') around signposts; grainy salt-and-pepper roads; Hill Country oaks W of Austin
Bollard = **white square reflector atop a black rectangle** (unique); streets named like **"600 South"**
Small **green signs, 3 rows of numbers**, 2nd row starts with **0** (New York's starts with a NYSDOT district #)
Bollard = white/brown post, **white rectangle on front + two small squares on back**; golden wheat fields east
Street signs with a **fraction-in-a-circle** symbol
Simple **white bollards w/ black reflectors** (unique); green **ATV Route** signs; orange rectangle+diamond pole plate (also MI UP)
Bollard: dark post, white circular reflector w/ post rising **above** it; top sometimes painted **white/grey** (unique)
**Interstate 19** (Tucson → Mexico) is the only US highway signed in **kilometres** (speed still MPH)
**Ford Bronco** (unblurred black roof, red hood) on unpaved roads = TransAmerica Trail promo, OR→NC
**Devil's Tower** (lone butte, NE Wyoming) visible for miles; Badlands/Theodore Roosevelt = sand-brown eroded rock
Pacific island nation NE of Brisbane; all coverage = **Gen3 trekker**
Volcanic island → **dark volcanic soil**; tropical, much coverage in dense forest
Of 13 islands, only **Ambrym Island** has coverage (find the 'Port Vato' label, N of Port Vila); a unique volcano trekker is in the centre
E of the volcano: a plateau w/ dark brown soil, then jungle w/ ferns (man in a red cap), then a **black-sand beach** on the east coast

hints.text: **Shitcam** exists in a few spots — with the same bone-shaped blur (other shitcam countries have a bigger circular blur); only in parts of the **Red River Delta**
**Grain drying on roads**
**Addresses on storefronts** run specific→general: number → ward/commune (phường/xã) → district (quận/huyện/thị xã/thành phố) → province (tỉnh) — often abbreviated (H., TP., TB.)
**Provinces** = the key address part — learn names + abbreviations (TG = Tien Giang)
bullets: 2025 reform merged most provinces — coverage predates it, so OLD province names are NOT scannable on the map → memorise them

hints.text: **Area codes** start with 2 (+1–2 digits), ordered north → south; **plate codes** (often painted on truck sides) map to regions; some **place-name prefixes** are regional

**Red River Delta**: wide, completely flat fields (less commonly Mekong); single **graves in the middle of fields**; boxy tall multi-storey houses with **Greco-Roman kitsch** (pillars, pediments — sometimes near HCMC too)
**Mekong Delta**: extremely flat & water-rich, dead-straight canals with linear villages, dense population, even more tropical (palms, wetland); houses boxy but **single-storey**
**Central Highlands**: hilly w/ distant mountains, **red soil**, dry rolling fields, drier vegetation; **black-pepper plantations** (vines shrouding tree trunks) in/around the highlands

**Crop metas**: rubber plantations (thin trunks w/ plastic wrap, dense grid) → north of HCMC + Central Highlands; tea (trimmed rows on hills) → north + Bảo Lộc/Lâm Đồng; khasi-pine clusters → far NE (Lạng Sơn, Quảng Ninh); dragonfruit (wet-mop look) → Phan Thiết + Long An/Tiền Giang border

Coast NE of Phan Thiết: noticeably **dry, sandy, partly rocky**; **wind-turbine clusters** mostly in the south (SE of Dalat, N of Buon Ma Thuot, S of Pleiku, SE of Dong Hoi, E of Quy Nhon, offshore S of HCMC, W-Quang Tri)
**North**: thin roof spires (almost only northern half); NW: traditional **wooden stilt houses** w/ corrugated roofs + triangular roof openings; grid-pattern **stick/bamboo fences** (like NE India)

**Pole region metas**: full-length holey poles → north (half-length = everywhere); few/no pinholes → north; trapezoid poletops → centre+north (3 off-centre incomplete trapezoids → north); triangle poletop w/ supports under crossbar → south; A-shape above crossbar → centre; uneven/off-centre crossbars → south; bamboo poles → rural NW mountains; black pole stickers (Malaysia-like, simpler) → north
Countryside densely covered in **greenhouses**
**Đồng Văn Karst Geopark** (far north): rounded pointy mountains w/ sparse vegetation + exposed **dark rock** (the wider north has pointy mountains too — the exposed rock is the tell)
Island near Cambodia: **Gen3 only**, dark red soil, forested ridge in the NE
Coastal roads east of Hải Phòng: **pointy rocky islands** rising from the sea
A **single large mountain** in an otherwise perfectly flat landscape (NE of the city)
Northern peninsula: **sand dunes hugging the road** (mostly divided highway)
Historic town south of Da Nang: **old yellow buildings** with dark brown doors/details
Mountain resort town: touristy stores, excessive signage, **palace-like grand hotels** (European-SEA style mix)
Beach resorts east of the city: signs with **Cyrillic** alongside Vietnamese (Russian tourists)
